content.problemStatement:
Kajaria has an empty bag and 2 types of tiles - tiles of type 111 have the number XXX written and those of type 222 have the number YYY written on them. He has an infinite supply of both types of tiles.

In one move, Kajaria adds exactly 111 tile to the bag. He adds a tile of type 111 with probability ppp and a tile of type 222 with probability (1−p)(1 - p)(1−p).

If 222 tiles in the bag have the same number written on them (say ZZZ), they are merged into a single tile of twice that number (2Z2Z2Z). Find the expected number of moves to reach the first tile with number SSS written on it.

**Notes on merging**:


Consider that the bag contains tiles (5,10,20,40)(5, 10, 20, 40)(5,10,20,40) and if the new tile added is 555, then it would merge with the existing 555 and the bag would now contain (10,10,20,40)(10, 10, 20, 40)(10,10,20,40). The tiles 101010 (already present) and 101010 (newly formed) would then merge in the same move to form (20,20,40)(20, 20, 40)(20,20,40), and that will form (40,40)(40, 40)(40,40), which will form (80)(80)(80).
Kajaria guarantees that:


XXX and YYY are not divisible by each other.
A tile with number SSS can be formed.

Input Format

First line contains a single integer TTT - the total no. of testcases.
Each testcase is described by 222 lines:

X,Y,SX, Y, SX,Y,S - 333 space-separated natural numbers.
u,vu, vu,v - 222 space-separated natural numbers describing the probability ppp.
The value of ppp is provided as a fraction in its lowest form u/vu/vu/v (uuu and vvv are co-prime).




Output Format

For each testcase, if the expected number of moves can be expressed as a fraction p/qp/qp/q in its lowest form, print (p⋅q−1)(p \cdot q^{-1})(p⋅q−1) modulo 109+710^9 + 7109+7, where q−1q^{-1}q−1 denotes the modular inverse of qqq wrt 109+710^9 + 7109+7.
